exercises.title: Trace the shapes
exercises.prompt: For a network that maps a batch of 4 samples with 3 features through a hidden layer of 5 units to 2 outputs, write the shape of every weight matrix and every intermediate activation. State the matmul shape rule.
exercises.hint: For X (batch, in) and W (in, out), X@W is (batch, out). Bias is (out,) broadcast over the batch.
exercises.solution:
```
X:        (4, 3)
W1:       (3, 5)   b1: (5,)   ->  H = X@W1 + b1 : (4, 5)
W2:       (5, 2)   b2: (2,)   ->  Y = H@W2 + b2 : (4, 2)
```
```python
import numpy as np
X=np.random.rand(4,3); W1=np.random.rand(3,5); W2=np.random.rand(5,2)
print((X@W1).shape, ((X@W1)@W2).shape)   # (4,5) (4,2)
```

Rule: `(a,b) @ (b,c) -> (a,c)` — the inner dimensions must match and cancel. The #1 deep-learning bug is a transposed weight (inner dims don't match); tracing shapes layer by layer catches it before runtime.

exercises.title: Linear stack collapses
exercises.prompt: Empirically show that two stacked linear layers (no activation) are equivalent to a single linear layer, then show that inserting a nonlinearity breaks that equivalence. Explain the consequence for depth.
exercises.hint: Compose W2(W1 x) = (W2 W1) x — a single matrix. Add a ReLU between them and the composition is no longer a single matmul.
exercises.solution:
```python
import numpy as np
W1=np.random.rand(3,4); W2=np.random.rand(4,2); x=np.random.rand(5,3)
linear_stack = (x@W1)@W2
single = x@(W1@W2)
print(np.allclose(linear_stack, single))   # True — collapses

relu=lambda z: np.maximum(0,z)
nonlinear = relu(x@W1)@W2
print(np.allclose(nonlinear, x@(W1@W2)))    # False — genuinely deeper
```

Without a nonlinearity, any number of linear layers reduces to one matrix `W2W1` — depth buys nothing. The ReLU (or any nonlinearity) between layers is what lets each layer bend space differently, so a deep net can represent curved decision boundaries a single layer never could.

exercises.title: Hand-tune a 2-2-1 XOR net
exercises.prompt: Build a 2-2-1 network with sigmoid activations and hand-set weights so it computes XOR. Verify all four inputs give the right side of 0.5. Explain what the two hidden units learned to detect.
exercises.hint: Make the hidden units act like OR and NAND (large weights for sharp sigmoids), then the output as AND of those.
